chapters.title: Having a life design is the key
chapters.text:
Everything around you is a product of design. Think of the most usual things surrounding you, like smartphones, computers, cars, houses, and clothes. Each of these things, which make life easier and more pleasurable, came to be because someone or a group of people somewhere sought to solve a problem.

At the core of each outstanding design lies a problem. For example, the inability to travel a long distance without getting dragged in a carriage led to the inception of fast and comfortable automobiles. You have a portable laptop with reliable battery life and a phone that fits perfectly in your hand because there was a problem that needed a solution.

Everything we see worldwide today attests to the benefits of a design-oriented approach to problems. You can use the same thinking that shaped the best technology and products to design the life and career you want for yourself.

Design thinking can bring fulfillment to your life.

Constant creation is intrinsic to life. Designers change the world by composing novel things. You, too, can be a designer. You can focus your creation on your ideal future. Life design can improve things that seem almost perfect.

A well-designed life is all about harmony; this concerns your identity, beliefs, and whatever you do. It is a life filled with great experiences of adventures, achievements, failures, and hardships. With a well-designed life, you’ll be conscious of where you're going, why you chose that direction, and how you can get there.

Figure out what a well-designed life looks like for you. Create a life plan to become who you want to be.

chapters.title: The vitality of knowing where you are
chapters.text:
Design thinking can help you create the future you want regardless of your life journey. Knowing your position and design concerns should precede establishing the goal of your journey.

Designers have a special relationship with the issue that is hard to tackle. They view them as exciting challenges. These are known as “wicked problems.” You probably have one or two wicked concerns in your life at the moment. Adopting the designer thinking style, you’ll address the issues with a completely different attitude.

Difficulties shouldn’t always upset you — some may excite you and keep your inspiration going.

Design thinking values problem-finding as much as issue-solving. There’s not much sense in dealing with irrelevant concerns. Deciding which worries are worth your effort is one of the most important decisions ever. Most people waste a lifetime or years of their lives solving irrelevant — “gravity” — issues. Gravity problems are predicaments that are not actionable or solvable; they are simply situations, circumstances, and facts of life.

For example, if you want to become a doctor but have an issue with dedicating 10-15 years of your life to the training required to be a physician, then you have a gravity problem. You’ll only get around this issue if you change the current medical education system. And to change the current system, you need to have a Doctor of Medicine (M.D.) degree.

One way to get stuck permanently in life is to focus on gravity problems. There is essentially nothing you can do about something that is not actionable. So, the best approach to gravity problems is acceptance. You have to start building your life from where you are, not where you hope you are. And to know where you are, you must assess your current situation.

To start, break your life into four distinct areas:

• Health
• Work
• Play
• Love

Reflect on each area of your life and rank them from zero to one hundred, with zero being the least important. Then, try to find an actionable problem in each segment you can tackle.

chapters.title: Articulating your life compass will bring you clarity
chapters.text:
Now that you know your starting point, it’s time to build a compass that will point you in the right direction for the journey ahead. A life compass has two components: a workview and a lifeview.

Workview determines the meaning of work to you. Take 30 minutes to reflect on the following questions:

• What is work for me?
• Why do I do it?
• What qualities does great work have?

After brainstorming, write down the ideas you came up with — no more than 250 words. Having established your work philosophy, you will dictate your own rules rather than being led by someone else.

Lifeview determines the meaning of the world for you. To better understand your lifeview, think about these questions for 30 minutes.

• What makes life meaningful to me?
• What gives value to my life?
• What is the relationship between my life and the way the world lives?
• What is the place of development and fulfillment in my life?

After you’ve reflected on your lifeview, write down your thoughts in 250 words or so.

According to Bill Burnett and Dave Evans, to find direction in life, we must establish coherence between our workview and lifeview. That is, who we are, what we believe, and what we do must align. It, in effect, will lead to a life of discontentment. For example, suppose your lifeview has to do with protecting animal rights, and you work for a corporation that produces makeup and tests all of it on animals. In that case, you’ll suffer because your job will go against your beliefs. It will probably make you miserable.

Some tricks can help you determine whether your lifeview and workview are consistent.

• Are my work and life coherent with each other?
• Are there any points of conflict?
• How do they inspire each other?

You can create your inner compass — True North — by ensuring that your workview and lifeview go hand in hand. You can check whether they align at any point in your life. You can always recalibrate your compass if you feel some disbalance.

Your True North — the product of the harmony between your values and work — will help you know whether you are on the right path.

chapters.title: Find out what works for you
chapters.text:
Now, your inner compass is ready. It’s finally time to start finding your direction. Wayfinding means determining your direction in life. While our compass gives us a sense of direction in life, we have no GPS that can help us pinpoint our ultimate destination. Instead, we all have to rely on clues and make our best way forward with the available resources. The most important clues that we need to monitor are our engagement and energy.

Engagement. Bill Burnett and Dave Evans recommend keeping a Good Time Journal — a diary that can help you catch yourself having a good time. You want to record how you feel during each of your day-to-day activities. By doing this, you will discover the things that reliably engage you.

Life design isn’t just about redesigning a whole new life; it is also about getting more out of your current life. ~ Bill Burnet

Energy. We engage in all sorts of physical and mental activities daily, and each of these actions either sustains our energy or drains it. Human beings need energy to live and thrive. Logging whatever you are doing in the Good Time Journal is essential for understanding which activities drain you and which bring you joy. This way, you’ll understand how your energy levels operate. It will help you plan your week better. This firm structure will increase your productivity and will make you feel better. You’ll know when you have the most energy to tackle challenging problems and when to rest.

If you have anxiety, journaling can become a life savior. It is a powerful tool for managing worries, allowing you to express your thoughts and emotions in a safe and private space. It helps you gain clarity, identify triggers, and develop coping strategies.

The things we dedicate our attention to establish our energy levels.

In essence, by tracking when you are engaged and energized and when you are not, you can focus on what you are doing and figure out what is working for you and what is not. It will ensure you don’t waste time doing things that get you nowhere regarding your life design.

chapters.title: To get unstuck, generate lots of ideas
chapters.text:
Feeling stuck from time to time is natural for the human condition. What most of us don’t know, however, is that being stuck is a great opportunity to be creative. Successful designers understand this secret and leverage it all the time.

Most people get stuck and never find a way out because they put all their valuable resources — time and energy — into pursuing a single idea. When we put all our eggs in one basket by focusing on a single way out of a situation, we invariably put a lot of pressure on ourselves, making it difficult to make logical decisions.

Whenever you find yourself stuck, the way out is to accept the problem, get over it, and then ideate — come up with many wild, crazy ideas and possibilities. Make sure you have many options for many possible futures. Don’t make the mistake of focusing solely on the first idea that comes to your mind or what you believe to be a perfect idea.

As a life designer, you must ideate wildly — come up with crazy ideas as much as possible.

Mind mapping is one way to constantly ideate and ultimately get unstuck.

Mind mapping consists of four steps:

• Decide on a topic. Go through your Good Time Journal and choose a case based on the experience that engages and energizes you.
• Make the mind map. Write down your topic in the center of a blank sheet of paper and circle it. Draw four lines, in different directions, from that circle. Write four things related to that central idea at the end of each line. Then draw four lines from each of the new points and write down four words associated with it. This step is complete when you have five levels of word associations.
• Make secondary connections and create concepts. Look at the associations you’ve compiled. Choose the most interesting ones. Combine them and create a few concepts.

The efficiency of mind mapping is so good because it goes beyond your logic and calls to your visual perception. To increase the effectiveness of this technique, never spend more than five minutes on it. Note down all the words you think about at each level.

chapters.title: The direction you’re heading in is just one of many
chapters.text:
When planning your life, you might tend to work with the mindset that there is one possible life you must work toward. The truth is, the reality you are currently experiencing is just one of many possible realities. Your life is a compilation of many other lives.

You are not limited to just one life; you can recreate your life design anywhere.

You need an Odyssey Plan if you want to make a life design. You must devise three life paths in the next five years to do this.

Your first life should focus on what you already do.

Your second life should be what you would do if your plans for the first life suddenly stopped working out. So, what would you do if the life you are living now is no longer an option?

The design of your third life depends on how you would live if money and other people’s opinions weren’t relevant.

One of the most powerful ways to design your life is to design your lives. ~ Dave Evans

After you’ve come up with the three alternative lives, give each of them a descriptive title that is no more than six words, and write down three questions that arise when you consider each version. For instance, the title of one of your life versions might be: “Creating Community — One Drink at a Time!” Questions like these may arise:

• Is this much risk worth it for me?
• Can the idea even work out?
• Will this bring me any profit?

Next, rank each choice for the following factors:

• Resources. Do you have the money, skills, and contacts to bring the plan to fruition?
• Likability. How much do you like the project?
• Confidence. How confident are you in your ability to pull this off?
• Coherence. Is the plan consistent with your lifeview and workview?

Finally, find two to five people you deem highly reliable close to you and share the three versions of you with them. Let them know beforehand that you don’t need their criticism or advice. You want them to listen, contemplate, and elaborate on your words by asking good, supportive questions.

chapters.title: Happiness should be your priority
chapters.text:
Whatever our current circumstances, our primary goal is happiness regardless of who we are or where we are. Everyone craves happiness. And the only way we can be satisfied, according to Bill Burnett and Dave Evans, is to choose joy.

The choosing process in life design can be overwhelming. Fortunately, it can be broken down into four stages:

Stage 1. Consider your options.

Up to this point in the summary, we’ve highlighted many option-generating tools. Practices such as having good insight about yourself, assessing your lifeview and workview, creating mind maps, and planning alternative lives can help you generate options in many areas of your life. Doing these exercises should leave you with a hefty list of options.

Stage 2. Prioritize.

Having too many choices is just like having none at all. What’s the point in having many possibilities if you are too overwhelmed to pick one to start working on? So, reassess all available opportunities and cross your non-top alternatives off the list. If you have a list of twelve options, cross out seven. Rewrite your list with the remaining five and move to the next stage.

Stage 3. Choose discerningly.

Discernment is a decision-making process that harnesses the power of our intuitive, emotional, and spiritual ways of knowing. Instead of simply choosing what seems logical, we go a step further by integrating all our decision-making faculties.

When we make discerning decisions, we bolster our intuition by listening to our gut or heart and practicing spiritual exercises like prayer, meditation, or even yoga.

Stage 4. Let go. Don’t agonize.

Once chosen, don’t fall into the rut of dreaming about what could have been. Instead of rehearsing the other alternatives and wondering if you’ve made the right choice, simply let go and move on.

Choosing happiness is all about seeing the adventure in the life we are building and living.

Did you know? According to a 2005 study by psychologists at the University of California and the University of Missouri, happiness is neither genetically fixed nor demands an external event or an accomplishment. We can always choose to be happy.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
By thinking like designers, we can stop living out other people’s scripts of a good life and create our ideal life.

The starting point is to figure out what problems you are trying to solve. And to do this, you need to assess the most critical sectors of your life. How are you doing in love, health, work, and play? Are there any problems in these areas that you must tackle immediately? Taking stock of your current situation lets you know where to focus your energy.

The next step is to calibrate your compass by ensuring that your lifeview and workview align. What work means to you shouldn’t contradict your ideas about the world. Doing something that aligns with who you are and what you believe in is essential for your peace of mind, contentment, and sense of direction.

If you find yourself stuck at any point, use that situation as an opportunity to get creative. Use mind mapping to develop different ideas that can lead you to a way forward. Remember: you are a multifaceted human with many lives within you.

Try this



• Start keeping a Good Time Journal. Do it for about three weeks. Note the activities that made you feel engaged and energized.
• Review the journal after three weeks and choose an activity you were engaged in and something you felt highly energized from. Create a mind map for each of these activities.
• Pick two ideas that resonate with you the most, research those ideas, and find ways to bring them to life.

Read it till the end?
